1:
"And there went a man of the house of Levi, and took to wife a daughter of Levi." The witnesses note the Levite marriage of Moses' parents. On "there went a man of the house of Levi," the grammatical voices note the parents. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "there went a man of the house of Levi, and took to wife a daughter of Levi" — a man of the tribe of Levi (Amram, Exodus 6:20) took to wife a daughter of Levi (Jochebed); the parents of Moses (and Aaron and Miriam); the Levite house (the priestly tribe); the godly parents (commended for their faith, Hebrews 11:23).

On "a man of the house of Levi," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the tribe. Calvin and Ellicott note the Levite descent (Amram and Jochebed, of the house of Kohath, Exodus 6:18-20) — the priestly tribe; the parents of the deliverer; the godly stock.

On "took to wife a daughter of Levi," the puritan Henry and grammatical-historical note the providence: the marriage (Amram and Jochebed) — the providence preparing the birth of Moses (the deliverer); the godly parents (who would hide the child by faith, v.2; Hebrews 11:23); the line of Levi from which the lawgiver and the priesthood would come (Exodus 6:20). The verse displays the Levite marriage of Moses' parents.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the parents — "a man of the house of Levi, and took to wife a daughter of Levi," Amram and Jochebed (Exodus 6:20, Keil, Gill); "a man of the house of Levi," the tribe (Calvin, Ellicott); the providence (Hebrews 11:23, Henry, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the Levite parents. The verse marks the man: of Levi. The verse displays the wife: of Levi. The verse displays the parents: of Moses. The verse displays the providence: preparing. The marriage displays the godly parents of the deliverer. The verse displays the Levite: married. The verse displays the deliverer: to be born. The verse displays the providence: at work. The Levite marriage of Moses' parents displays the providence preparing the deliverer: a man of Levi took a daughter of Levi (Exodus 6:20; Hebrews 11:23) — Amram and Jochebed, the godly Levite parents of Moses. The verse displays the birth of the goodly child (v.2).
2:
"And the woman conceived, and bare a son: and when she saw him that he was a goodly child, she hid him three months." The witnesses note the birth of Moses and his hiding by faith. On "the woman conceived, and bare a son... that he was a goodly child," the grammatical voices note the child. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "the woman conceived, and bare a son: and when she saw him that he was a goodly child, she hid him three months" — Jochebed bore a son (Moses); seeing he was a "goodly" (fair, beautiful) child — the "exceeding fair" (Acts 7:20, "fair to God"), perhaps marking him as one set apart by God; she hid him three months (defying Pharaoh's edict, Exodus 1:22); the hiding of the child.

On "a goodly child," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the beauty. Calvin and Ellicott note the "goodly" child (Acts 7:20, "exceeding fair"; Hebrews 11:23, "a proper child") — the special beauty (perhaps a token of God's favor, moving the parents' faith); the child marked out.

On "she hid him three months," the puritan Henry and the dominant reading note the faith: "she hid him three months" — the parents, by faith, hid the child (defying the king's edict, Exodus 1:22, not fearing the king's wrath); the faith specially commended — "By faith Moses, when he was born, was hid three months of his parents, because they saw he was a proper child; and they were not afraid of the king's commandment" (Hebrews 11:23); the courage of faith (the fear of God above the fear of the king, as the midwives, Exodus 1:17); the providence preserving the deliverer. The verse displays the birth of Moses and his hiding by faith.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the child — "bare a son... a goodly child, she hid him three months," the goodly child (Acts 7:20, Keil, Gill); "a goodly child," the beauty (Hebrews 11:23, Calvin, Ellicott); "she hid him three months," the faith (Hebrews 11:23; Exodus 1:17, Henry, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the hidden Moses. The verse marks the child: goodly. The verse displays the hiding: three months. The verse displays the faith: of the parents. The verse displays the edict: defied. The hiding displays the parents' faith preserving the child. The verse displays the Moses: born. The verse displays the parents: by faith. The verse displays the providence: preserving. The birth of Moses and his hiding by faith display the courage of faith: she bare a goodly child and hid him three months (Acts 7:20; Hebrews 11:23) — the parents, by faith, defying the king's edict, the deliverer preserved. The verse displays the ark of bulrushes (v.3).
3:
"And when she could not longer hide him, she took for him an ark of bulrushes, and daubed it with slime and with pitch, and put the child therein; and she laid it in the flags by the river's brink." The witnesses note Moses placed in the ark of bulrushes. On "she took for him an ark of bulrushes, and daubed it with slime and with pitch," the grammatical voices note the ark. Ellicott and Gill note that "she took for him an ark of bulrushes, and daubed it with slime and with pitch, and put the child therein" — Jochebed, unable to hide Moses longer (the child growing, the danger increasing), made a little ark (a basket, a chest) of bulrushes (papyrus), waterproofed with slime (bitumen) and pitch, and placed the child in it; the providence (the ark, like Noah's, a vessel of preservation through the water, Genesis 6:14, the same word "pitch"); the faith committing the child to God.

On "an ark of bulrushes," the reformed and witnesses note the vessel. Gill notes the "ark" (the basket of papyrus, the word echoing Noah's ark, Genesis 6:14) — the little vessel of preservation; the faith that, having done what it could, committed the child to God's care.

On "she laid it in the flags by the river's brink," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the trust: "she laid it in the flags by the river's brink" — the ark laid among the reeds at the river's edge (where Pharaoh's daughter would come, v.5); the faith (committing the child to the very river of death, Exodus 1:22, trusting God to preserve him); the providence (the place chosen, by God's ordering, where the child would be found and saved, vv.5-10). The verse displays Moses placed in the ark of bulrushes.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the ark — "she took for him an ark of bulrushes... and put the child therein," the vessel (Genesis 6:14, Ellicott, Gill); "an ark of bulrushes," the basket (Gill); "she laid it in the flags by the river's brink," the trust (vv.5-10, Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the ark of Moses. The verse marks the ark: of bulrushes. The verse displays the pitch: waterproofed. The verse displays the child: placed within. The verse displays the river: the brink. The ark displays the faith committing the child to God. The verse displays the Jochebed: making the ark. The verse displays the Moses: within. The verse displays the providence: at work. Moses placed in the ark of bulrushes displays the faith committing the child to God: she made an ark of bulrushes and laid it by the river (Genesis 6:14; Exodus 1:22) — the little vessel of preservation, the child committed to God at the river's brink. The verse displays the watching sister (v.4).
4:
"And his sister stood afar off, to wit what would be done to him." The very brief verse notes Miriam watching over the ark. The grammatical voices note the sister. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "his sister stood afar off, to wit what would be done to him" — Moses' sister (Miriam, Exodus 15:20; Numbers 26:59) stood at a distance to watch what would happen to the child; the loving care of the sister; the watching (that she might act, as she did, v.7-8, to bring the mother as nurse); the providence using the sister's vigilance.

On "his sister stood afar off," the witnesses note the watch. Gill notes Miriam's watching (from a distance, to see the child's fate) — the loving vigilance; the sister set to watch; the care of the family.

On "to wit what would be done to him," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the providence: "to wit what would be done to him" — Miriam watching to know the child's fate (and ready to act); the providence (the sister's watching that would bring the mother to nurse the child, vv.7-9); the care that God would use to preserve and restore the child. The verse displays Miriam watching over the ark.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the watch — "his sister stood afar off, to wit what would be done to him," Miriam watching (Numbers 26:59, Keil, Gill); "his sister stood afar off," the vigilance (Gill); "to wit what would be done to him," the providence (vv.7-9, Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the watching sister. The verse marks the sister: Miriam. The verse displays the watch: afar off. The verse displays the care: loving. The verse displays the providence: at work. The watch displays Miriam's loving vigilance. The verse displays the Miriam: watching. The verse displays the ark: observed. The verse displays the providence: using her. Miriam watching over the ark displays the loving vigilance: his sister stood afar off to watch (Numbers 26:59; vv.7-9) — the care that God would use to bring the mother to nurse the child. The verse displays Pharaoh's daughter at the river (v.5).
5:
"And the daughter of Pharaoh came down to wash herself at the river; and her maidens walked along by the river's side; and when she saw the ark among the flags, she sent her maid to fetch it." The witnesses note Pharaoh's daughter finding the ark. On "the daughter of Pharaoh came down to wash herself at the river," the grammatical voices note the providence. Henry and Gill note that "the daughter of Pharaoh came down to wash herself at the river... and when she saw the ark among the flags, she sent her maid to fetch it" — Pharaoh's daughter came to the river to bathe, saw the ark among the reeds, and sent her maid to fetch it; the providence (the very daughter of the king who decreed the death, by God's ordering, coming to the spot, finding and saving the child); the divine direction of events (the child found by the one who could protect him).

On "she saw the ark among the flags," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and witnesses note the finding. Wesley and Clarke note the finding of the ark (by Pharaoh's daughter, the very court that sought the child's death) — the providence; the child found by the one who would save him; the divine overruling of the edict.

On "she sent her maid to fetch it," the reformed and grammatical-historical note the rescue: "she sent her maid to fetch it" — the ark fetched (the child about to be found, v.6); the providence (the deliverer drawn from the river of death, raised in the very palace of the oppressor); the irony (Moses nursed by his own mother, v.8-9, and reared as Pharaoh's daughter's son, v.10, at Pharaoh's own cost); God overruling the plot to preserve and prepare the deliverer. The verse displays Pharaoh's daughter finding the ark.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the providence — "the daughter of Pharaoh came down... and when she saw the ark among the flags, she sent her maid to fetch it," the finding (Henry, Gill); "she saw the ark among the flags," the providence (Wesley, Clarke); "she sent her maid to fetch it," the rescue (vv.6-10, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the finding princess. The verse marks the daughter: of Pharaoh. The verse displays the ark: found. The verse displays the providence: directing. The verse displays the rescue: begun. The finding displays the providence saving the child. The verse displays the princess: finding. The verse displays the ark: fetched. The verse displays the deliverer: rescued. Pharaoh's daughter finding the ark displays the providence overruling the edict: Pharaoh's daughter saw the ark and sent her maid to fetch it (vv.6-10) — the child saved by the very court that sought his death, the deliverer drawn from the river. The verse displays the weeping child (v.6).
6:
"And when she had opened it, she saw the child: and, behold, the babe wept. And she had compassion on him, and said, This is one of the Hebrews' children." The witnesses note Pharaoh's daughter's compassion on the weeping babe. On "when she had opened it, she saw the child: and, behold, the babe wept," the grammatical voices note the discovery. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "when she had opened it, she saw the child: and, behold, the babe wept" — Pharaoh's daughter opened the ark and saw the child, and the babe wept; the tears of the infant (moving her pity); the discovery of the Hebrew child; the providence (the babe's weeping stirring the princess's compassion).

On "she had compassion on him," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and witnesses note the pity. Clarke and Ellicott note the princess's compassion (her pity moved by the weeping babe) — the providence using a woman's tenderness to save the child; the compassion overruling the edict (the king's daughter pitying the child the king would drown); the mercy that preserved the deliverer.

On "This is one of the Hebrews' children," the reformed and grammatical-historical note the recognition: "This is one of the Hebrews' children" — the princess recognizing the child as a Hebrew (under the edict, Exodus 1:22); yet, in compassion, saving him; the providence (the child known to be a Hebrew, yet spared and reared in the palace); the deliverer preserved by the oppressor's daughter. The verse displays Pharaoh's daughter's compassion on the babe.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the compassion — "she saw the child... the babe wept. And she had compassion on him," the pity (Keil, Gill); "she had compassion on him," the tenderness (Clarke, Ellicott); "This is one of the Hebrews' children," the recognition (Exodus 1:22, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the compassionate princess. The verse marks the babe: wept. The verse displays the compassion: moved. The verse displays the Hebrew: recognized. The verse displays the providence: at work. The compassion displays the princess pitying the child. The verse displays the princess: compassionate. The verse displays the babe: spared. The verse displays the providence: saving. Pharaoh's daughter's compassion on the babe displays the mercy that preserved the deliverer: she saw the weeping babe and had compassion, This is a Hebrew child (Exodus 1:22) — the king's daughter pitying the child the king would drown. The verse displays the offered nurse (vv.7-9).
7:
"Then said his sister to Pharaoh's daughter, Shall I go and call to thee a nurse of the Hebrew women, that she may nurse the child for thee?" The very brief verse notes Miriam offering to fetch a Hebrew nurse. The grammatical voices note the offer. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "his sister said to Pharaoh's daughter, Shall I go and call to thee a nurse of the Hebrew women, that she may nurse the child for thee?" — Miriam (watching, v.4), seeing the princess's compassion, stepped forward and offered to fetch a Hebrew nurse for the child; the ready wit of the sister; the providence (the offer that would bring the child's own mother to nurse him, vv.8-9); the loving care.

On "Shall I go and call to thee a nurse of the Hebrew women," the witnesses note the providence. Gill notes Miriam's offer (to fetch a Hebrew nurse) — the providence (the very plan to restore the child to his mother); the wit and care of the sister; the divine ordering.

On the offer, the reformed and grammatical-historical note the restoration: Miriam's offer — the means (by God's providence) of restoring Moses to his own mother (to be nursed, vv.8-9, and reared, at Pharaoh's cost); the care that God used; the deliverer given back to his godly home for his early years. The verse displays Miriam offering to fetch a Hebrew nurse.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the offer — "Shall I go and call to thee a nurse of the Hebrew women," the offer (vv.8-9, Keil, Gill); "a nurse of the Hebrew women," the providence (Gill); the restoration (vv.8-9, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the offering sister. The verse marks the offer: a Hebrew nurse. The verse displays the Miriam: stepping forward. The verse displays the providence: the restoration. The verse displays the mother: to be called. The offer displays Miriam's wit restoring the child. The verse displays the sister: offering. The verse displays the nurse: to be fetched. The verse displays the providence: at work. Miriam offering to fetch a Hebrew nurse displays the providence restoring the child: shall I call a nurse of the Hebrew women? (vv.8-9) — the offer that would bring the child's own mother to nurse him. The verse displays the called mother (v.8).
8:
"And Pharaoh's daughter said to her, Go. And the maid went and called the child's mother." The very brief verse notes the child's own mother called as nurse. The grammatical voices note the providence. Ellicott and Gill note that "Pharaoh's daughter said to her, Go. And the maid went and called the child's mother" — the princess agreed, and Miriam went and called the child's own mother (Jochebed); the providence (the mother brought to nurse her own child, by the princess's leave, at the palace's cost); the restoration of the child to his mother; the divine ordering.

On "Go," the witnesses note the leave. Gill notes the princess's leave (sending Miriam to fetch the nurse) — the providence; the way opened for the mother.

On "the maid went and called the child's mother," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the restoration: "the maid went and called the child's mother" — Jochebed called to nurse her own son (the providence restoring the child to his mother); the irony (the mother paid to nurse her own child, vv.9, by the oppressor's daughter); God overruling all to preserve and prepare the deliverer in his own godly home. The verse displays the child's own mother called as nurse.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the providence — "Pharaoh's daughter said to her, Go. And the maid went and called the child's mother," the restoration (Ellicott, Gill); "Go," the leave (Gill); "the maid went and called the child's mother," Jochebed (vv.9, Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the called mother. The verse marks the leave: Go. The verse displays the mother: called. The verse displays the providence: restoring. The verse displays the child: to his mother. The calling displays the child restored to his mother. The verse displays the princess: agreeing. The verse displays the Jochebed: called. The verse displays the providence: at work. The child's own mother called as nurse displays the providence restoring the child: the maid called the child's mother (vv.9) — Jochebed brought to nurse her own son, by the princess's leave. The verse displays the nursing charge (v.9).
9:
"And Pharaoh's daughter said unto her, Take this child away, and nurse it for me, and I will give thee thy wages. And the woman took the child, and nursed it." The very brief verse notes Jochebed paid to nurse her own son. The grammatical voices note the charge. Gill and Ellicott note that "Pharaoh's daughter said unto her, Take this child away, and nurse it for me, and I will give thee thy wages. And the woman took the child, and nursed it" — the princess charged Jochebed to nurse the child for her, promising wages; Jochebed took her own son and nursed him; the providence (the mother given back her child, and paid to raise him, by the very court that sought his death); the deliverer reared in his own godly home for his early, formative years.

On "nurse it for me, and I will give thee thy wages," the witnesses note the providence. Gill notes the wages (Jochebed paid to nurse her own child) — the irony and the providence (God restoring the child and providing for him through the oppressor); the divine overruling.

On "the woman took the child, and nursed it," the reformed and grammatical-historical note the restoration: "the woman took the child, and nursed it" — Jochebed nursing her own son (the early years in the godly home, where she would teach him his people and his God, the foundation of the faith by which Moses would later refuse Egypt, Hebrews 11:24-26); the providence preparing the deliverer. The verse displays Jochebed paid to nurse her own son.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the charge — "nurse it for me, and I will give thee thy wages. And the woman took the child, and nursed it," the restoration (Gill, Ellicott); "I will give thee thy wages," the providence (Gill); "the woman took the child, and nursed it," the godly home (Hebrews 11:24, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the nursing mother. The verse marks the charge: nurse it. The verse displays the wages: paid. The verse displays the mother: nursing. The verse displays the providence: restoring. The charge displays the child restored and provided for. The verse displays the Jochebed: nursing. The verse displays the child: in the home. The verse displays the providence: at work. Jochebed paid to nurse her own son displays the providence restoring and providing: nurse it for me, and the woman took the child and nursed it (Hebrews 11:24-26) — the mother given back her child and paid to raise him, the deliverer reared in the godly home. The verse displays the named Moses (v.10).
10:
"And the child grew, and she brought him unto Pharaoh's daughter, and he became her son. And she called his name Moses: and she said, Because I drew him out of the water." The witnesses note Moses adopted by Pharaoh's daughter and named. On "the child grew, and she brought him unto Pharaoh's daughter, and he became her son," the grammatical voices note the adoption. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "the child grew, and she brought him unto Pharaoh's daughter, and he became her son" — Moses, weaned, was brought to Pharaoh's daughter and adopted as her son; the providence (the Hebrew child reared in the Egyptian palace, "learned in all the wisdom of the Egyptians," Acts 7:22, prepared for his future task); the deliverer in the court of the oppressor.

On "he became her son," the reformed JFB and witnesses note the providence. JFB and Calvin note Moses adopted (the son of Pharaoh's daughter) — the providence (the deliverer reared and educated in the palace, yet keeping the faith of his mother, Hebrews 11:24); the strange ordering (the oppressor's house nurturing Israel's deliverer).

On "she called his name Moses: and she said, Because I drew him out of the water," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the name: "she called his name Moses... Because I drew him out of the water" — the name Moses (Egyptian "mesu," son/born; or Hebrew, "drawn out") given because he was drawn out of the water; the name a memorial of his deliverance (and a type, the fathers note, of him who would draw Israel out of the waters, and of Christ, drawn up from death); the deliverer named for his being drawn from the water. The verse displays Moses adopted and named.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the adoption — "she brought him unto Pharaoh's daughter, and he became her son," the adoption (Acts 7:22, Keil, Gill); "he became her son," the providence (Hebrews 11:24, JFB, Calvin); "she called his name Moses... Because I drew him out of the water," the name (Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the adopted Moses. The verse marks the child: grown. The verse displays the adoption: her son. The verse displays the name: Moses. The verse displays the providence: at work. The adoption displays Moses reared in the palace. The verse displays the Moses: adopted. The verse displays the name: drawn out. The verse displays the deliverer: prepared. Moses adopted by Pharaoh's daughter and named displays the providence preparing the deliverer: he became her son, named Moses, drawn out of the water (Acts 7:22; Hebrews 11:24) — the Hebrew child reared in the palace, named for his deliverance. The verse displays the grown Moses, who visits his brethren (v.11).
11:
"And it came to pass in those days, when Moses was grown, that he went out unto his brethren, and looked on their burdens: and he spied an Egyptian smiting an Hebrew, one of his brethren." The witnesses note Moses, grown, going out to his brethren — the crux of his faith and choice. On "when Moses was grown, that he went out unto his brethren, and looked on their burdens," interpreters expound the choice. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note that Moses, now grown (forty years old, Acts 7:23), "went out unto his brethren, and looked on their burdens" — Moses, though reared as Pharaoh's daughter's son in all the wealth and honor of Egypt, went out to his enslaved brethren the Hebrews and looked on (with sympathy, identifying himself with) their burdens; the great choice — Moses, by faith, owning his afflicted people and his God rather than the honors of Egypt; the faith specially commended: "By faith Moses, when he was come to years, refused to be called the son of Pharaoh's daughter; choosing rather to suffer affliction with the people of God, than to enjoy the pleasures of sin for a season; esteeming the reproach of Christ greater riches than the treasures in Egypt" (Hebrews 11:24-26); the deliberate, faith-filled choice of the suffering people of God over the world.

On "he spied an Egyptian smiting an Hebrew," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and witnesses note the occasion. Wesley and Clarke note that Moses saw an Egyptian beating a Hebrew (one of his brethren) — the occasion of his action (v.12); the sympathy with his oppressed people; the indignation at the wrong (the first stirring of the deliverer's zeal).

On the going out, the puritan Henry and grammatical-historical note the identification: Moses' going out to his brethren and owning their burdens — the proof of his faith (identifying with the despised, enslaved people of God, esteeming their reproach above Egypt's treasures, Hebrews 11:26); the choice that prepared the deliverer; the faith that, though Moses' zeal then outran God's time (vv.12-15), was the right and commended choice of God's people over the world. The verse displays Moses, grown, going out to his brethren.

The witnesses agree the substance: Moses, grown, went out to his brethren and looked on their burdens, seeing an Egyptian smite a Hebrew (Calvin, Wesley, Henry, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of his faith and choice: Moses, reared in Pharaoh's palace, by faith owning his afflicted brethren and his God rather than the honors of Egypt — the great choice commended in Hebrews 11:24-26 (refusing to be called Pharaoh's daughter's son, choosing affliction with God's people, esteeming the reproach of Christ above Egypt's treasures); the identification with the suffering people of God that marked the deliverer. The verse displays the faith-and-choice crux. The verse marks the going: to his brethren. The verse displays the burdens: looked on. The verse displays the faith: owning his people. The verse displays the choice: God's people over Egypt. The going out displays Moses' faith choosing his afflicted people. The verse displays the Moses: going out. The verse displays the brethren: owned. The verse displays the faith: commended. Moses, grown, going out to his brethren displays the crux: he went out to his brethren and looked on their burdens (Acts 7:23; Hebrews 11:24-26) — Moses by faith owning his afflicted people and his God rather than the honors of Egypt, the great choice of God's people over the world. The verse displays the avenging Moses (v.12).
12:
"And he looked this way and that way, and when he saw that there was no man, he slew the Egyptian, and hid him in the sand." The witnesses note Moses slaying the Egyptian. On "he looked this way and that way, and when he saw that there was no man," the grammatical voices note the deed. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "he looked this way and that way, and when he saw that there was no man, he slew the Egyptian, and hid him in the sand" — Moses, seeing the Egyptian smiting the Hebrew (v.11), looked about, and finding none watching, killed the Egyptian and hid the body in the sand; the deed (defending his brother); the deliverer's zeal (smiting the oppressor); the act done secretly.

On "he slew the Egyptian," interpreters weigh the deed. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note Moses' act — variously regarded: (a) by faith, as a deliverer beginning his work (Acts 7:24-25, "he supposed his brethren would have understood how that God by his hand would deliver them"; Moses acting as the appointed deliverer, avenging his oppressed brother, his zeal commended in substance though premature); (b) yet the manner (the private killing, the haste, before God's time and call) is generally held a fault — Moses' zeal outrunning God's time and method (the right cause, but the wrong way, taking the matter into his own hand, leading to his flight, v.15, and the forty years' delay in Midian); the witnesses balance: Moses' zeal for his people was right and God-given, but the rash, private deed was premature, and God would call him in his own time (ch. 3) to deliver by God's power, not man's violence.

On "hid him in the sand," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the secrecy: "hid him in the sand" — Moses hiding the body (the deed secret, yet to be known, v.14); the providence (the deed that drove Moses to Midian, where God would meet and call him, ch. 3); the deliverer's premature zeal overruled to prepare him in the wilderness. The verse displays Moses slaying the Egyptian.

The witnesses agree the substance: Moses, seeing the wrong, killed the Egyptian and hid him (Keil, Calvin, Wesley, Gill). The deed bears a balance: Moses' zeal for his oppressed brethren was right and God-given (Acts 7:24-25, acting as the appointed deliverer), yet the rash, private killing, before God's time and call, is generally held premature and faulty — the right cause in the wrong way, leading to his flight and the forty years' preparation in Midian; God would deliver in his own time by his own power. The verse displays the slaying of the Egyptian. The verse marks the deed: the Egyptian slain. The verse displays the zeal: for his brethren. The verse displays the haste: premature. The verse displays the providence: overruling. The deed displays Moses' zeal, right but premature. The verse displays the Moses: slaying. The verse displays the Egyptian: hidden. The verse displays the flight: to come. Moses slaying the Egyptian displays the zeal right but premature: he slew the Egyptian and hid him (Acts 7:24-25) — the deliverer's God-given zeal for his brethren, yet the rash deed before God's time, leading to Midian. The verse displays the striving Hebrews (v.13).
13:
"And when he went out the second day, behold, two men of the Hebrews strove together: and he said to him that did the wrong, Wherefore smitest thou thy fellow?" The witnesses note Moses rebuking the Hebrew wrongdoer. On "two men of the Hebrews strove together," the grammatical voices note the strife. Ellicott and Gill note that "when he went out the second day, behold, two men of the Hebrews strove together: and he said to him that did the wrong, Wherefore smitest thou thy fellow?" — Moses, going out again, found two Hebrews fighting, and rebuked the one in the wrong (the aggressor) — "why smitest thou thy fellow?"; the effort to reconcile his brethren; the deliverer seeking to be a peacemaker; the rebuke of the wrongdoer.

On "he said to him that did the wrong," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the rebuke. Calvin and Clarke note Moses' rebuke (to the one in the wrong) — the attempt to make peace among his brethren; the deliverer's concern for justice among his own; the rebuke that would be rejected (v.14).

On "Wherefore smitest thou thy fellow?" the grammatical-historical note the peacemaking: "Wherefore smitest thou thy fellow?" — Moses' appeal (why wrong thy brother, thy fellow Hebrew?); the peacemaking effort; the rejection to come (v.14, the wrongdoer turning on Moses); the deliverer rejected by his own (as Christ, John 1:11; Acts 7:25-27). The verse displays Moses rebuking the Hebrew wrongdoer.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the strife — "two men of the Hebrews strove together: and he said to him that did the wrong, Wherefore smitest thou thy fellow?" the rebuke (Ellicott, Gill); "he said to him that did the wrong," the rebuke (Calvin, Clarke); "Wherefore smitest thou thy fellow?" the peacemaking (Acts 7:26, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the rebuking Moses. The verse marks the strife: two Hebrews. The verse displays the rebuke: the wrongdoer. The verse displays the peacemaking: attempted. The verse displays the rejection: to come. The rebuke displays Moses seeking peace among his brethren. The verse displays the Moses: rebuking. The verse displays the wrongdoer: reproved. The verse displays the rejection: coming. Moses rebuking the Hebrew wrongdoer displays the peacemaking deliverer: wherefore smitest thou thy fellow? (Acts 7:26) — Moses seeking to reconcile his brethren, soon rejected by his own. The verse displays the rejecting Hebrew (v.14).
14:
"And he said, Who made thee a prince and a judge over us? intendest thou to kill me, as thou killedst the Egyptian? And Moses feared, and said, Surely this thing is known." The witnesses note the wrongdoer rejecting Moses, and Moses' fear. On "Who made thee a prince and a judge over us?" the grammatical voices note the rejection. Ellicott and Gill note that "he said, Who made thee a prince and a judge over us? intendest thou to kill me, as thou killedst the Egyptian?" — the wrongdoer turned on Moses, rejecting his authority ("who made thee a prince and a judge?") and revealing that the killing of the Egyptian (v.12) was known; the rejection of the deliverer by his own (Acts 7:27-28, "But he that did his neighbour wrong thrust him away"); the ingratitude and unbelief of the brethren.

On "Who made thee a prince and a judge over us?" the reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the type. Calvin and the fathers note the rejection — the brethren refusing Moses as ruler and judge (Acts 7:35, "This Moses whom they refused... the same did God send to be a ruler and a deliverer"); a striking type of Christ, rejected by his own (John 1:11; the very words echoed of Christ, "who made thee a ruler?"; Luke 12:14); the deliverer spurned by the people he came to save.

On "Moses feared, and said, Surely this thing is known," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the fear: "Moses feared, and said, Surely this thing is known" — Moses, finding the deed known (and so his life in danger from Pharaoh, v.15), feared; the fear that drove him to flee (v.15); the providence (the rejection and flight that sent Moses to Midian, to be prepared by God for forty years, ch. 3). The verse displays the wrongdoer rejecting Moses, and Moses' fear.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the rejection — "Who made thee a prince and a judge over us?... as thou killedst the Egyptian?" the rejection (Acts 7:27, Ellicott, Gill); "Who made thee a prince and a judge over us?" the type (Acts 7:35; John 1:11, Calvin, the fathers); "Moses feared... Surely this thing is known," the fear (v.15, Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the rejected Moses. The verse marks the rejection: who made thee. The verse displays the deed: known. The verse displays the fear: of Moses. The verse displays the type: of Christ. The rejection displays Moses spurned by his own. The verse displays the wrongdoer: rejecting. The verse displays the Moses: fearing. The verse displays the flight: to come. The wrongdoer rejecting Moses, and Moses' fear, display the deliverer spurned by his own: who made thee a prince? and Moses feared (Acts 7:27, 35; John 1:11) — the deliverer rejected by the people he would save, a type of Christ, driven to flee. The verse displays the fleeing Moses (v.15).
15:
"Now when Pharaoh heard this thing, he sought to slay Moses. But Moses fled from the face of Pharaoh, and dwelt in the land of Midian: and he sat down by a well." The witnesses note Moses fleeing to Midian. On "when Pharaoh heard this thing, he sought to slay Moses," the grammatical voices note the danger. Ellicott and Gill note that "when Pharaoh heard this thing, he sought to slay Moses. But Moses fled from the face of Pharaoh, and dwelt in the land of Midian" — Pharaoh, hearing of the killing (v.12) and Moses' siding with the Hebrews, sought to kill him; Moses fled to Midian (in the Sinai peninsula or Arabia); the flight (by faith, Hebrews 11:27, not fearing the king, or, the danger past, removing himself); the deliverer in exile.

On "Moses fled from the face of Pharaoh, and dwelt in the land of Midian," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the exile. Calvin and Clarke note Moses' flight to Midian (forsaking Egypt, Hebrews 11:27, "By faith he forsook Egypt, not fearing the wrath of the king") — the long exile (forty years, Acts 7:30); the providence (the wilderness years preparing Moses, humbling the man of Egypt's court into the shepherd, fitting him for his task, ch. 3); the deliverer in the school of the desert.

On "he sat down by a well," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the providence: "he sat down by a well" — Moses resting by a well in Midian (the place of his meeting with Reuel's daughters, vv.16-21); the providence (the well where God would provide him a home and wife); the exile that prepared the deliverer. The verse displays Moses fleeing to Midian.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the flight — "Pharaoh... sought to slay Moses. But Moses fled... and dwelt in the land of Midian," the exile (Hebrews 11:27, Ellicott, Gill); "Moses fled from the face of Pharaoh," the forsaking of Egypt (Acts 7:30, Calvin, Clarke); "he sat down by a well," the providence (vv.16-21, Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the fleeing Moses. The verse marks the danger: Pharaoh's wrath. The verse displays the flight: to Midian. The verse displays the well: where he sat. The verse displays the providence: preparing. The flight displays Moses in exile, prepared by God. The verse displays the Moses: fleeing. The verse displays the Midian: his refuge. The verse displays the deliverer: in the desert. Moses fleeing to Midian displays the exile preparing the deliverer: Moses fled from Pharaoh and dwelt in Midian (Hebrews 11:27; Acts 7:30) — the forty years' exile humbling and preparing the deliverer in the school of the desert. The verse displays the daughters at the well (vv.16-17).
16:
"Now the priest of Midian had seven daughters: and they came and drew water, and filled the troughs to water their father's flock." The witnesses note the seven daughters of the priest of Midian at the well. On "the priest of Midian had seven daughters," the grammatical voices note the family. Henry and Gill note that "the priest of Midian had seven daughters: and they came and drew water, and filled the troughs to water their father's flock" — the priest (or prince) of Midian (Reuel/Jethro, vv.18; Exodus 3:1) had seven daughters who came to draw water for their father's flock; the pastoral scene (the daughters as shepherdesses); the providence (the meeting that would give Moses a home and wife, vv.17-21).

On "the priest of Midian," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note Reuel. Calvin and Ellicott note the priest of Midian (Reuel, or Jethro, a priest or prince, perhaps of the true God, a descendant of Abraham through Keturah, Genesis 25:2) — Moses' future father-in-law; the godly (or partly godly) household.

On "they came and drew water, and filled the troughs," the grammatical-historical note the scene: "they came and drew water, and filled the troughs to water their father's flock" — the daughters watering the flock (the pastoral life); the well-scene (like Rebekah, Genesis 24; Rachel, Genesis 29; the providence at the well); the meeting that would provide Moses a home (vv.17-21). The verse displays the seven daughters at the well.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the family — "the priest of Midian had seven daughters... to water their father's flock," the daughters (Henry, Gill); "the priest of Midian," Reuel (Genesis 25:2, Calvin, Ellicott); "they came and drew water," the well-scene (Genesis 24; 29, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the daughters at the well. The verse marks the priest: of Midian. The verse displays the daughters: seven. The verse displays the well: the water. The verse displays the providence: at work. The scene displays the meeting at the well of Midian. The verse displays the daughters: drawing water. The verse displays the flock: watered. The verse displays the providence: preparing. The seven daughters at the well display the providence preparing Moses a home: the priest of Midian's daughters came to draw water (Genesis 24; 29) — the well-scene where Moses would find his home and wife. The verse displays Moses helping them (v.17).
17:
"And the shepherds came and drove them away: but Moses stood up and helped them, and watered their flock." The witnesses note Moses defending the daughters and watering their flock. On "the shepherds came and drove them away," the grammatical voices note the wrong. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "the shepherds came and drove them away: but Moses stood up and helped them, and watered their flock" — when shepherds came and drove the daughters from the well, Moses stood up, defended them, and watered their flock; the chivalry of Moses (defending the weak against the strong); the same zeal for justice (as with the Egyptian and the Hebrews, vv.11-13), now rightly used; the helpfulness of the exile.

On "Moses stood up and helped them," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the help. Calvin and Wesley note Moses' help (defending the daughters, watering the flock) — the kindness and courage; the standing for the wronged; the character of the deliverer (a helper of the oppressed).

On "watered their flock," the grammatical-historical note the providence: "watered their flock" — Moses watering the flock (the kind service); the providence (the help that would lead to Reuel's hospitality and Moses' marriage, vv.18-21); the exile finding a home through his kindness. The verse displays Moses defending the daughters.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the help — "the shepherds came and drove them away: but Moses stood up and helped them, and watered their flock," the defense (Keil, Gill); "Moses stood up and helped them," the kindness (Calvin, Wesley); "watered their flock," the providence (vv.18-21, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the helping Moses. The verse marks the shepherds: driving them. The verse displays the Moses: standing up. The verse displays the flock: watered. The verse displays the providence: at work. The help displays Moses defending the weak. The verse displays the Moses: helping. The verse displays the daughters: defended. The verse displays the providence: preparing. Moses defending the daughters and watering their flock displays his chivalry and the providence: Moses stood up and helped them (vv.18-21) — defending the weak against the strong, the kindness that would find him a home. The verse displays the daughters' report (vv.18-19).
18:
"And when they came to Reuel their father, he said, How is it that ye are come so soon to day?" The very brief verse notes the daughters returning early to Reuel. The grammatical voices note the return. Gill and Wesley note that "when they came to Reuel their father, he said, How is it that ye are come so soon to day?" — the daughters returned to their father Reuel (Jethro, Exodus 3:1) sooner than usual (Moses' help having sped their watering); Reuel's question (surprised at their early return); the occasion of the inquiry (vv.19-20) that would bring Moses to the household.

On "Reuel their father," the witnesses note the priest. Gill notes Reuel (also called Jethro, Exodus 3:1, and Raguel, Numbers 10:29; the priest/prince of Midian) — the father of the seven daughters; Moses' future father-in-law.

On "How is it that ye are come so soon to day?" the reformed and grammatical-historical note the inquiry: "How is it that ye are come so soon to day?" — Reuel's surprise (the early return, owing to Moses' help, v.17); the question that drew out the account (vv.19) and led to Moses' welcome (vv.20-21); the providence bringing Moses to the home. The verse displays the daughters returning early to Reuel.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the return — "they came to Reuel their father... How is it that ye are come so soon to day?" the early return (Gill, Wesley); "Reuel their father," the priest (Exodus 3:1, Gill); "How is it that ye are come so soon to day?" the inquiry (vv.19-20, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the returning daughters. The verse marks the Reuel: their father. The verse displays the return: early. The verse displays the inquiry: how so soon. The verse displays the providence: at work. The return displays the daughters come home early. The verse displays the daughters: returning. The verse displays the Reuel: questioning. The verse displays the providence: preparing. The daughters returning early to Reuel display the providence bringing Moses to the home: how is it ye are come so soon? (vv.19-20) — the early return, owing to Moses' help, drawing out the account that brought him to the household. The verse displays the daughters' report (v.19).
19:
"And they said, An Egyptian delivered us out of the hand of the shepherds, and also drew water enough for us, and watered the flock." The very brief verse notes the daughters reporting Moses' help. The grammatical voices note the report. Ellicott and Gill note that "they said, An Egyptian delivered us out of the hand of the shepherds, and also drew water enough for us, and watered the flock" — the daughters reported how an "Egyptian" (Moses, taken for an Egyptian by his dress and speech) had delivered them from the shepherds and watered their flock; the account of Moses' help (v.17); the occasion of Reuel's hospitality (vv.20-21).

On "An Egyptian delivered us," the witnesses note the mistake. Gill notes that Moses was taken for an Egyptian (by his Egyptian dress, manner, or speech, reared in the palace) — the daughters not knowing he was a Hebrew; the report of the stranger's kindness.

On "drew water enough for us, and watered the flock," the grammatical-historical note the help: "drew water enough for us, and watered the flock" — Moses' generous help (drawing water, watering the flock); the kindness reported; the occasion (Reuel's desire to reward and welcome him, vv.20-21). The verse displays the daughters reporting Moses' help.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the report — "An Egyptian delivered us... and watered the flock," the account (v.17, Ellicott, Gill); "An Egyptian delivered us," the mistake (Gill); "drew water enough for us, and watered the flock," the help (vv.20-21, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the reporting daughters. The verse marks the Egyptian: Moses taken for. The verse displays the deliverance: from the shepherds. The verse displays the flock: watered. The verse displays the providence: at work. The report displays the daughters telling of Moses' help. The verse displays the daughters: reporting. The verse displays the Moses: the helper. The verse displays the providence: preparing. The daughters reporting Moses' help display the occasion of his welcome: an Egyptian delivered us and watered the flock (v.17) — the account of the stranger's kindness that drew Reuel's hospitality (vv.20-21). The verse displays Reuel's call (v.20).
20:
"And he said unto his daughters, And where is he? why is it that ye have left the man? call him, that he may eat bread." The very brief verse notes Reuel calling Moses to hospitality. The grammatical voices note the welcome. Ellicott and Gill note that "he said unto his daughters, And where is he? why is it that ye have left the man? call him, that he may eat bread" — Reuel, hearing of Moses' kindness (v.19), rebuked his daughters for leaving the stranger unthanked and bade them call him to eat bread (to a meal, the token of hospitality and welcome); the hospitality of Reuel; the welcome that would give Moses a home (v.21).

On "why is it that ye have left the man?" the witnesses note the hospitality. Gill notes Reuel's concern (that the kind stranger be welcomed, not left) — the duty of hospitality (Hebrews 13:2); the gracious welcome.

On "call him, that he may eat bread," the grammatical-historical note the welcome: "call him, that he may eat bread" — Reuel's invitation (to a meal, the beginning of Moses' dwelling with him, v.21); the providence (the hospitality that gave Moses a home, a wife, and forty years' preparation in Midian); the welcome of the exile. The verse displays Reuel calling Moses to hospitality.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the welcome — "where is he?... call him, that he may eat bread," the hospitality (v.19, Ellicott, Gill); "why is it that ye have left the man?" the concern (Hebrews 13:2, Gill); "call him, that he may eat bread," the welcome (v.21, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the hospitable Reuel. The verse marks the welcome: call him. The verse displays the bread: a meal. The verse displays the hospitality: Reuel's. The verse displays the home: to be given. The welcome displays Reuel's hospitality to Moses. The verse displays the Reuel: calling. The verse displays the Moses: welcomed. The verse displays the providence: at work. Reuel calling Moses to hospitality displays the welcome of the exile: call him, that he may eat bread (Hebrews 13:2; v.21) — the hospitality that gave Moses a home and forty years' preparation in Midian. The verse displays Moses dwelling with Reuel (v.21).
21:
"And Moses was content to dwell with the man: and he gave Moses Zipporah his daughter." The very brief verse notes Moses dwelling with Reuel and marrying Zipporah. The grammatical voices note the home. Ellicott and Gill note that "Moses was content to dwell with the man: and he gave Moses Zipporah his daughter" — Moses agreed to dwell with Reuel (Jethro), and Reuel gave him his daughter Zipporah to wife; the home found in Midian; the marriage (Zipporah, the mother of Gershom and Eliezer, Exodus 18:3-4); the providence settling the exile.

On "Moses was content to dwell with the man," the witnesses note the settling. Gill notes Moses' contentment (to dwell in Midian, the exile finding a home) — the providence; the shepherd-life (forty years, Acts 7:30) that would humble and prepare him.

On "he gave Moses Zipporah his daughter," the reformed and grammatical-historical note the marriage: "he gave Moses Zipporah his daughter" — Moses' marriage to Zipporah (the Midianite, daughter of the priest); the providence (the home, the wife, the years of preparation in the desert, fitting Moses, the man of Egypt's court, into the humble shepherd whom God would call, ch. 3). The verse displays Moses dwelling with Reuel and marrying Zipporah.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the home — "Moses was content to dwell with the man: and he gave Moses Zipporah his daughter," the settling (Ellicott, Gill); "Moses was content to dwell with the man," the home (Acts 7:30, Gill); "he gave Moses Zipporah his daughter," the marriage (Exodus 18:3, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the settled Moses. The verse marks the dwelling: with Reuel. The verse displays the Zipporah: his wife. The verse displays the home: found. The verse displays the providence: at work. The settling displays Moses finding a home in Midian. The verse displays the Moses: content. The verse displays the marriage: to Zipporah. The verse displays the providence: preparing. Moses dwelling with Reuel and marrying Zipporah displays the providence settling the exile: Moses dwelt with Reuel, who gave him Zipporah (Acts 7:30; Exodus 18:3) — the home and wife in Midian, the years preparing the deliverer. The verse displays the son Gershom (v.22).
22:
"And she bare him a son, and he called his name Gershom: for he said, I have been a stranger in a strange land." The witnesses note the birth and naming of Gershom. On "she bare him a son, and he called his name Gershom," the grammatical voices note the son. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "she bare him a son, and he called his name Gershom: for he said, I have been a stranger in a strange land" — Zipporah bore Moses a son, named Gershom ("a stranger there," or "exile"); the name commemorating Moses' condition as a stranger and exile in Midian; the testimony of his pilgrim state.

On "I have been a stranger in a strange land," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and witnesses note the pilgrim. Wesley and Calvin note Moses' word — "a stranger in a strange land": Moses owning himself an exile and pilgrim (in Midian, far from his people); the pilgrim spirit of the godly (strangers on earth, looking for the homeland, Hebrews 11:13-16); the faith that, even in exile, held to the covenant hope; the stranger far from the promised land.

On the name, the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the testimony: Gershom (the name of exile) — Moses' testimony to his pilgrim state (a stranger in Midian, his heart still with his people and the promise); the faith that did not forget Israel and the covenant (the deliverer waiting, in exile, for God's time, vv.23-25); the pilgrim hope. The verse displays the birth and naming of Gershom.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the son — "she bare him a son, and he called his name Gershom... I have been a stranger in a strange land," the naming (Keil, Gill); "I have been a stranger in a strange land," the pilgrim (Hebrews 11:13, Wesley, Calvin); the testimony (vv.23-25, Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the naming Moses. The verse marks the son: Gershom. The verse displays the name: a stranger. The verse displays the exile: owned. The verse displays the pilgrim: spirit. The naming displays Moses' testimony to his exile. The verse displays the Moses: naming. The verse displays the Gershom: the exile. The verse displays the pilgrim: hope. The birth and naming of Gershom display Moses' pilgrim state: he named him Gershom, a stranger in a strange land (Hebrews 11:13-16) — the exile owning his pilgrim condition, his heart still with his people and the promise. The verse displays the groaning Israel (v.23).
23:
"And it came to pass in process of time, that the king of Egypt died: and the children of Israel sighed by reason of the bondage, and they cried, and their cry came up unto God by reason of the bondage." The witnesses note Israel's cry rising to God under the bondage. On "in process of time... the king of Egypt died," the grammatical voices note the time. Henry and Gill note that "in process of time, that the king of Egypt died: and the children of Israel sighed by reason of the bondage, and they cried" — after many years (Moses' forty in Midian, Acts 7:30), the king of Egypt died (yet the bondage continued, or grew worse); Israel, groaning under the bondage, cried out; the long affliction; the cry of the oppressed.

On "the children of Israel sighed by reason of the bondage, and they cried," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the cry. Calvin and Wesley note Israel's sighing and crying (under the cruel bondage) — the groaning of the oppressed; the cry wrung out by affliction (whether a cry to God in prayer, or the cry of misery that God heard); the affliction ripe for deliverance.

On "their cry came up unto God by reason of the bondage," the puritan Henry and grammatical-historical note the hearing: "their cry came up unto God" — the cry rising to God (who hears the cry of the afflicted, Psalm 12:5; the groaning that moved the providence toward deliverance); the turning-point (the affliction ripe, the time of the promise drawing near, Genesis 15:13-16); God about to act (vv.24-25). The verse displays Israel's cry rising to God under the bondage.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the cry — "the children of Israel sighed by reason of the bondage, and they cried, and their cry came up unto God," the groaning (Acts 7:30, Henry, Gill); "the children of Israel sighed... and they cried," the cry (Calvin, Wesley); "their cry came up unto God," the hearing (Psalm 12:5, Henry, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the crying Israel. The verse marks the king: died. The verse displays the bondage: groaned under. The verse displays the cry: to God. The verse displays the deliverance: drawing near. The cry displays Israel's groaning rising to God. The verse displays the Israel: crying. The verse displays the bondage: bitter. The verse displays the God: hearing. Israel's cry rising to God under the bondage displays the turning-point: their cry came up unto God by reason of the bondage (Psalm 12:5; Genesis 15:13-16) — the groaning of the oppressed rising to God, the affliction ripe, the deliverance drawing near. The verse displays the God who remembered (v.24).
24:
"And God heard their groaning, and God remembered his covenant with Abraham, with Isaac, and with Jacob." The witnesses note God hearing and remembering his covenant — the crux of the covenant-remembrance. On "God heard their groaning," the grammatical voices note the hearing. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "God heard their groaning, and God remembered his covenant" — God heard Israel's groaning under the bondage (v.23); the God who hears the cry of the afflicted (Psalm 34:17); the turning of God toward his people; the answer to the long cry.

On "God remembered his covenant with Abraham, with Isaac, and with Jacob," interpreters expound the remembrance. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note "God remembered his covenant" — God "remembered" his covenant with the patriarchs (Genesis 15:13-18; 17:7-8; 26:3; 28:13-15); not that God had ever forgotten (he cannot forget), but the language of accommodation: God "remembered" in the sense of now acting on the covenant, bringing to pass the promised deliverance at the appointed time (the four hundred years drawing to their close, Genesis 15:13-16); the covenant the ground of the deliverance (God delivering not for Israel's worth, but for his covenant and oath to the fathers, Deuteronomy 7:8; Psalm 105:8-10, 42); the faithfulness of God to his covenant (the sure ground of all his mercies); the turning-point of the Exodus — God remembering, and about to act (Exodus 3).

On the remembrance, the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the comfort: God's remembering his covenant — the comfort to the afflicted (God's covenant the sure ground of deliverance); the providence (the covenant kept, the deliverance begun, the answer to Joseph's dying faith, Genesis 50:24, "God will surely visit you"); the faithfulness of God moving to redeem his people. The verse displays God hearing and remembering his covenant.

The witnesses agree the substance: God heard Israel's groaning and remembered his covenant with the fathers (Keil, Calvin, Wesley, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of the covenant-remembrance: "God remembered his covenant" — not that God had forgotten, but the language of accommodation for his now acting on the covenant at the appointed time (Genesis 15:13-18), delivering Israel for his oath to the fathers (Deuteronomy 7:8; Psalm 105:8-10), the covenant the sure ground of the deliverance; the turning-point of the Exodus, the answer to Joseph's dying faith (Genesis 50:24), God's faithfulness moving to redeem. The verse displays the covenant-remembrance crux. The verse marks the hearing: the groaning. The verse displays the remembrance: the covenant. The verse displays the fathers: Abraham, Isaac, Jacob. The verse displays the deliverance: at hand. The remembrance displays God's faithfulness to his covenant. The verse displays the God: remembering. The verse displays the covenant: the ground. The verse displays the deliverance: beginning. God hearing and remembering his covenant displays the crux: God remembered his covenant with Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob (Genesis 15:13-18; Deuteronomy 7:8; Psalm 105:8-10) — not forgetting, but acting on the covenant at the appointed time, the faithfulness of God moving to redeem his people. The verse displays the God who looked upon them (v.25).
25:
"And God looked upon the children of Israel, and God had respect unto them." The witnesses note God looking upon Israel with regard. On "God looked upon the children of Israel," the grammatical voices note the regard. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "God looked upon the children of Israel, and God had respect unto them" — God looked upon (regarded, took notice of) the afflicted Israelites; the eye of God turning to his people in mercy; the divine notice of their suffering (the answer to the cry, v.23); the turning toward deliverance.

On "God looked upon the children of Israel," the reformed and witnesses note the mercy. Gill notes God's looking (with pity and care, as he looks on the afflicted, Psalm 25:18) — the merciful regard; the eye of God on his suffering people; the prelude to action.

On "God had respect unto them," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and grammatical-historical note the knowing: "God had respect unto them" (Hebrew, "God knew," took knowledge, regarded, acknowledged them) — God acknowledging his people (owning them in their affliction, knowing their sorrows, as Exodus 3:7, "I know their sorrows"); the four verbs of v.24-25 (God heard, remembered, looked, knew) marking the full turning of God toward the deliverance; the providence (God's regard moving to the call of Moses and the Exodus, ch. 3); the covenant God owning his afflicted people. The verse displays God looking upon Israel with regard.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the regard — "God looked upon the children of Israel, and God had respect unto them," the merciful notice (v.23, Keil, Gill); "God looked upon the children of Israel," the pity (Psalm 25:18, Gill); "God had respect unto them," the knowing (Exodus 3:7, Clarke, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the regarding God. The verse marks the looking: upon Israel. The verse displays the respect: God knew. The verse displays the mercy: turning. The verse displays the deliverance: at hand. The regard displays God owning his afflicted people. The verse displays the God: looking. The verse displays the Israel: regarded. The verse displays the deliverance: beginning. God looking upon Israel with regard displays the turning toward deliverance: God looked upon Israel and had respect (knew) them (Exodus 3:7; Psalm 25:18) — the covenant God owning his afflicted people, his mercy moving to the Exodus. The verse closes the chapter, before the call of Moses (ch.3).
